text:
The fastest man in history, Usain Bolt, has confessed he decided against going to the Paris 2024 Olympics due to injury concerns making him feel "weird" about being seen on crutches.

Despite his legendary status with eight Olympic golds and still holding three world records, Bolt had to withdraw his plans of attending this year's Games as a spectator. He suffered a nasty fall during a charity football match organised by Unicef on June 9, which led to a significant Achilles tendon injury.

Opening up about his choice to skip the event on the "High Performance" podcast, Bolt explained the rationale behind his decision: "I was supposed to go with my family, but I injured myself," and further clarified, "I was playing a charity game, a football match, for soccer for Unicef and I ruptured my Achilles. I didn't want to go on crutches. It's my first time going without and not competing, so it would look weird for every time you see me on TV I'm on crutches like this. So I was just like, you know what, let me just stay home."

Bolt shared that although he's retired from competition, the sting of missing the Games lingers, especially since he sat out of the crowdless Tokyo Olympics too, an event he had participated in since his Olympic debut in Athens in 2004, remarking how challenging he found being merely a spectator.

The Jamaican sprinting icon expressed a profound sense of longing for the track, recounting to AFP: "I really missed it. I was like, I wish I was there," and shared his conviction in his own abiding potential at 35, stating confidently that he could claim a medal in the men's 100m. "My coach said something to me at the end of my career. He said, 'People are not getting faster. I was getting slower.' I never looked at it that way," Bolt revealed, underscoring a truth about the pace of progress in sprinting.



Adding to his reflection on the state of athletics, and his own extraordinary benchmarks, he added: "And it's the facts because a lot of guys don't really get faster. Because I have pushed the barrier so far and then I started going backward time-wise, so for me, 9.80 was possible to get done."

In a stunning athletic display in Paris, USA's Noah Lyles seized victory in the men's 100m race, just shy of Bolt's momentous world record. Prior to this triumph, with ambitions high of surpassing Bolt's record by the 2024 Olympics, Lyles clocked a stupendous 9.79 seconds sprint for gold.



Yet even with this win, Lyles couldn't topple Bolt's staggering world record of 9.58, nor his Olympic best of 9.69.

Despite the intense competition, Bolt's legacy continues to wield a deep influence over contemporary racers. From afar, as triumphs unfolded without his participation, Bolt reached out to Lyles with a virtual nod to his enduring supremacy.

In posting images from his memorable wins at the Beijing Olympics in 2008, captioned "1 of 1", Bolt broadcasted a clear message: his legendary status remains unmatched.

title: Usain Bolt explains why he snubbed Olympic Games and refused TV appearances | Other | Sport | Express.co.uk
author: Joseph McBride
description: Usain Bolt was expected to attend the Paris 2024 Olympics with his family this summer before he ruptured his Achilles tendon while playing in a charity soccer match
